stories.headline: Coding agents can now review and revise their own pull requests
stories.summary: One-shot pull request generation has always been open-loop: propose, then stop. This arXiv preprint introduces SWE-Review, adding a reviewer agent that explores the repository, accepts or rejects the change, and feeds structured feedback into a second pass. The benchmark and training dataset are public. The generate-review-revise loop is now the credible architecture for coding agents handling issue resolution.

claims.claim: Coding agents can now review and revise their own pull requests
claims.summary_span: Coding agents can now review and revise their own pull requests
claims.source_span: We introduce SWE-Review, a framework for closing this loop with agentic code review. Given an issue and an AI-generated PR, a reviewer agent explores the repository, decides whether the PR should be accepted, and provides structured feedback for revision.
claims.note: The source confirms a framework enabling agentic review and revision of AI-generated PRs, supporting the headline's claim.

claims.claim: One-shot pull request generation has always been open-loop: propose, then stop.
claims.summary_span: One-shot pull request generation has always been open-loop: propose, then stop.
claims.source_span: one-shot PR generation remains open-loop: the PR is proposed without systematic review, diagnosis, or revision.
claims.note: The source explicitly states one-shot PR generation is open-loop, consistent with the summary's paraphrase.

claims.claim: This arXiv preprint introduces SWE-Review
claims.summary_span: This arXiv preprint introduces SWE-Review
claims.source_span: SWE-Review: Closing the Loop on Issue Resolution with Agentic Code Review
claims.note: The source is an arXiv paper (referenced via hf papers read 2607.06065) introducing SWE-Review; 'arXiv preprint' is a reasonable characterisation.

claims.claim: SWE-Review adds a reviewer agent that explores the repository
claims.summary_span: adding a reviewer agent that explores the repository
claims.source_span: a reviewer agent explores the repository, decides whether the PR should be accepted, and provides structured feedback for revision.
claims.note: Directly supported by the source.

claims.claim: The reviewer agent accepts or rejects the change
claims.summary_span: accepts or rejects the change
claims.source_span: decides whether the PR should be accepted
claims.note: Accepting or rejecting is a fair paraphrase of 'decides whether the PR should be accepted'.

claims.claim: The reviewer agent feeds structured feedback into a second pass
claims.summary_span: feeds structured feedback into a second pass
claims.source_span: provides structured feedback for revision
claims.note: 'Second pass' is a reasonable paraphrase of the revision step in the generate-review-revise loop.

claims.claim: The benchmark and training dataset are public.
claims.summary_span: The benchmark and training dataset are public.
claims.source_span:
Datasets citing this paper 2
SWE-Lego/SWE-Review-Traj
SWE-Lego/SWE-Review-Bench
claims.note: Both the benchmark (SWE-Review-Bench) and training dataset (SWE-Review-Traj) are listed as public datasets on the platform.

claims.claim: The generate-review-revise loop is now the credible architecture for coding agents handling issue resolution.
claims.summary_span: The generate-review-revise loop is now the credible architecture for coding agents handling issue resolution.
claims.source_span: These results position agentic code review as a practical mechanism for moving AI coding agents from one-shot PR generation toward closed-loop issue resolution.
claims.note: This is an editorial/forward-looking judgment paraphrasing the paper's conclusion; it is consistent with the source and not a factual divergence.

sections.name: big_picture

stories.headline: A third of US banks cannot stop an autonomous agent mid-task
stories.summary: Wolters Kluwer's May report finds more than one-third of US banks have no kill switch: a mechanism to pause or disable autonomous AI agents. Regulators are signalling this will be required. If your institution is deploying agentic AI, where does the shutdown authority sit in your governance structure?

stories.headline: Inference costs fell 50x a year, making your data layer the bottleneck
stories.summary: A UC Berkeley perspective piece argues that with inference prices dropping a median 50x per year, the binding constraint on agentic workloads shifts from compute to data architecture. Agents querying databases generate thousands of sub-queries per task, with 80–90% duplicating prior work. Is your data infrastructure designed for a single human analyst, or a swarm?

stories.headline: AI agents routinely grab excess permissions, and safety training won't stop them
stories.summary: An arXiv preprint introduces ToolPrivBench, a benchmark across eight domains, extending 07-06's attack-surface finding: mainstream agents consistently grab higher-privilege tools even when lower-privilege ones suffice. Transient failures amplify the pattern, and general safety alignment doesn't transfer to least-privilege behaviour. If alignment doesn't transfer to least-privilege behaviour, what does your governance framework substitute for it?

claims.claim: AI agents routinely grab excess permissions, and safety training won't stop them
claims.summary_span: AI agents routinely grab excess permissions, and safety training won't stop them
claims.source_span: over-privileged tool selection is common among mainstream LLM agents and is further amplified by transient failures. We further find that general safety alignment does not reliably transfer to least-privilege tool choice
claims.note: 'excess permissions' paraphrases 'over-privileged tool selection' and 'safety training won't stop them' paraphrases 'general safety alignment does not reliably transfer' — both are supported compressions.

claims.claim: An arXiv preprint introduces ToolPrivBench
claims.summary_span: An arXiv preprint introduces ToolPrivBench
claims.source_span: We introduce ToolPrivBench to evaluate whether agents choose higher-privilege tools despite sufficient lower-privilege alternatives
claims.note: The source is an arXiv submission that introduces ToolPrivBench.

claims.claim: ToolPrivBench is a benchmark across eight domains
claims.summary_span: a benchmark across eight domains
claims.source_span: Across eight domains and five recurring risk patterns
claims.note: Source confirms eight domains.

claims.claim: ToolPrivBench extends 07-06's attack-surface finding
claims.summary_span: extending 07-06's attack-surface finding
claims.note: The source excerpt makes no reference to a prior '07-06' finding or attack-surface work; this detail cannot be confirmed or denied from the excerpt.

claims.claim: Mainstream agents consistently grab higher-privilege tools even when lower-privilege ones suffice
claims.summary_span: mainstream agents consistently grab higher-privilege tools even when lower-privilege ones suffice
claims.source_span: over-privileged tool selection is common among mainstream LLM agents
claims.note: 'consistently grab higher-privilege tools even when lower-privilege ones suffice' is a supported paraphrase of 'over-privileged tool selection is common among mainstream LLM agents'.

claims.claim: Transient failures amplify the pattern
claims.summary_span: Transient failures amplify the pattern
claims.source_span: is further amplified by transient failures
claims.note: Direct match to source language.

claims.claim: General safety alignment doesn't transfer to least-privilege behaviour
claims.summary_span: general safety alignment doesn't transfer to least-privilege behaviour
claims.source_span: general safety alignment does not reliably transfer to least-privilege tool choice
claims.note: Near-verbatim paraphrase of the source; dropping 'reliably' is a minor compression, not a distortion.

stories.headline: Modal raises $355M on the argument that agents broke the old cloud
stories.summary: Modal CTO Akshat Bubna's case, in a Latent Space interview: the cloud was designed for developers who fill in missing context; agents can't. Modal's answer is sandboxes, elastic GPU bursting, and feedback loops fast enough for 100,000 isolated RL environments. For a compliance or fraud-detection RL deployment, is your cloud contract built around isolation granularity or raw GPU count?

sections.intro_lead: The stack has new weak points.
sections.intro_body: Across compute, data, permissions, and shutdown authority, agentic deployments are exposing structural gaps that vendors are racing to fill. The governance question is whether your institution gets there first.

sections.name: hands_on

stories.headline: Databricks finds token price misleads on coding agent cost
stories.summary: Databricks benchmarked coding agents on real internal pull requests across a multi-million-line codebase. The sharpest finding: Sonnet 5 cost more per task than Opus 4.8 despite cheaper tokens, consuming 1.9x more. Harness choice alone drove 2x cost swings at equal quality. Run task-level cost analysis on your own codebase; per-token pricing will mislead your model selection.

claims.claim: Databricks finds token price misleads on coding agent cost
claims.summary_span: Databricks finds token price misleads on coding agent cost
claims.source_span: Developers often eyeball token costs to determine how expensive a model will be when completing coding tasks. We found, however, that token costs are often a poor indicator of overall task costs
claims.note: Source confirms Databricks found token costs mislead on overall task cost.

claims.claim: Databricks benchmarked coding agents on real internal pull requests across a multi-million-line codebase.
claims.summary_span: Databricks benchmarked coding agents on real internal pull requests across a multi-million-line codebase.
claims.source_span: which evaluates tools on actual coding tasks our engineers performed on the Databricks codebase. Tasks featured edits against a multi-million line codebase
claims.note: Source confirms benchmark used real internal PRs against a multi-million line codebase.

claims.claim: Sonnet 5 cost more per task than Opus 4.8 despite cheaper tokens.
claims.summary_span: Sonnet 5 cost more per task than Opus 4.8 despite cheaper tokens
claims.source_span: Sonnet 5 is ~1.7x cheaper per token than Opus 4.8, but, on our tasks, we found that Sonnet cost $2.09/task vs Opus's $1.94
claims.note: Source confirms Sonnet 5 is cheaper per token but cost more per task than Opus 4.8.

claims.claim: Sonnet 5 consumed 1.9x more tokens.
claims.summary_span: consuming 1.9x more
claims.source_span: Sonnet 5 worked longer and read more to get there, consuming 1.9x more tokens
claims.note: Source directly states Sonnet 5 consumed 1.9x more tokens.

claims.claim: Harness choice alone drove 2x cost swings at equal quality.
claims.summary_span: Harness choice alone drove 2x cost swings at equal quality.
claims.source_span: when we ran the same model with the same thinking effort through two different harnesses (Claude Code/Codex vs Pi), we observed that the cost per task differed significantly (more than 2x in some cases), while quality remained the same
claims.note: Source confirms harness choice caused more than 2x cost difference at equal quality; 'alone' is a fair paraphrase of same model/same effort.

stories.headline: Hugging Face's kernel registry adds code signing and agent-ready build tooling
stories.summary: Hugging Face has redesigned Kernels: custom GPU kernels now live in a dedicated Hub repository type, load only from verified publishers by default, and are signed via Sigstore's ephemeral keys. A new cross-framework interface lets one kernel run across PyTorch, JAX, and CuPy. Pull `kernels` and `kernel-builder`; the signed-publisher default gates arbitrary code before it reaches your process.

claims.claim: Hugging Face's kernel registry adds code signing and agent-ready build tooling
claims.summary_span: Hugging Face's kernel registry adds code signing and agent-ready build tooling
claims.source_span: we have added additional layers of defense: trusted kernel publishers and code signing... kernel-builder and kernels complement the rise of agentic kernel development
claims.note: Source confirms Hugging Face added code signing and agentic kernel development tooling to the Kernels project on the Hub.

claims.claim: Hugging Face has redesigned Kernels
claims.summary_span: Hugging Face has redesigned Kernels
claims.source_span: we also almost completely redesigned the project
claims.note: Source confirms the project was almost completely redesigned.

claims.claim: custom GPU kernels now live in a dedicated Hub repository type
claims.summary_span: custom GPU kernels now live in a dedicated Hub repository type
claims.source_span: We have introduced a new repository type on the Hub called "kernel".
claims.note: Source confirms a new 'kernel' repository type on the Hub; 'GPU kernels' is a fair paraphrase of custom compute kernels.

claims.claim: kernels load only from verified publishers by default
claims.summary_span: load only from verified publishers by default
claims.source_span: the kernels package will now only load kernels by trusted publishers by default
claims.note: 'Verified publishers' is a fair paraphrase of 'trusted publishers'.

claims.claim: kernels are signed via Sigstore's ephemeral keys
claims.summary_span: signed via Sigstore's ephemeral keys
claims.source_span: we use Sigstore's cosign to sign using ephemeral private keys
claims.note: Source confirms Sigstore's cosign with ephemeral private keys is used for signing.

claims.claim: A new cross-framework interface lets one kernel run across PyTorch, JAX, and CuPy
claims.summary_span: A new cross-framework interface lets one kernel run across PyTorch, JAX, and CuPy
claims.source_span: Apache TVM FFI is the first framework to be supported besides Torch. TVM FFI is standardized ABI for kernels that interoperates with other frameworks such as PyTorch, Jax and CuPy. This allows kernel developers to make kernels that runs across frameworks.
claims.note: Source confirms TVM FFI enables cross-framework kernels across PyTorch, JAX, and CuPy; 'cross-framework interface' is a fair paraphrase.

claims.claim: Pull `kernels` and `kernel-builder`
claims.summary_span: Pull `kernels` and `kernel-builder`
claims.source_span: Kernel signing is already supported by kernel-builder and we have provided the kernels verify-signature to verify a kernel.
claims.note: Both 'kernels' and 'kernel-builder' are named packages in the source; the call to pull them is editorial but the names are accurate.

claims.claim: the signed-publisher default gates arbitrary code before it reaches your process
claims.summary_span: the signed-publisher default gates arbitrary code before it reaches your process
claims.source_span: Kernels run native code with the same privileges as the Python process that loads them, so a malicious kernel can do real harm... the kernels package will now only load kernels by trusted publishers by default
claims.note: Source confirms the trusted-publisher default prevents untrusted code from loading into the Python process.

stories.headline: OpenAI flags reliability problems in a widely used coding benchmark
stories.summary: OpenAI's own analysis finds SWE-Bench Pro, a benchmark many teams use to compare coding agents, has reliability and accuracy problems. That makes any model-selection decision anchored to it shakier than it looks. Before your next coding-agent procurement, pull the benchmark methodology and pressure-test which eval you're actually trusting.

stories.headline: NVIDIA open-sources over 10 trillion agent-training tokens with an interactive data map
stories.summary: NVIDIA's Nemotron release puts more than 10 trillion pre-training tokens and millions of post-training samples into the open, alongside a visual Prompt Atlas mapping each sample by domain, tool use, and pipeline stage. The Personas dataset now covers 2.4 billion people across ten countries. Load the Prompt Atlas; use domain filters to find failure-recovery gaps before your next fine-tuning run.

claims.claim: NVIDIA open-sources over 10 trillion agent-training tokens with an interactive data map
claims.summary_span: NVIDIA open-sources over 10 trillion agent-training tokens with an interactive data map
claims.source_span: we've released over 10 trillion pre-training tokens and millions of post-training samples spanning many domains and data shapes... we built the Nemotron Post-Training v3 Prompt Atlas: an interactive visual map
claims.note: Source confirms 10 trillion+ tokens released openly and an interactive visual map; 'agent-training' is a fair characterisation of the post-training data focus.

claims.claim: NVIDIA's Nemotron release puts more than 10 trillion pre-training tokens and millions of post-training samples into the open
claims.summary_span: NVIDIA's Nemotron release puts more than 10 trillion pre-training tokens and millions of post-training samples into the open
claims.source_span: we've released over 10 trillion pre-training tokens and millions of post-training samples spanning many domains and data shapes
claims.note: Near-verbatim match with the source.

claims.claim: alongside a visual Prompt Atlas mapping each sample by domain, tool use, and pipeline stage
claims.summary_span: a visual Prompt Atlas mapping each sample by domain, tool use, and pipeline stage
claims.source_span: Color overlays and filters let you reorganize the map by dataset, pipeline stage, domain, or tool use
claims.note: Source confirms the Prompt Atlas filters by domain, tool use, and pipeline stage.

claims.claim: The Personas dataset now covers 2.4 billion people across ten countries
claims.summary_span: The Personas dataset now covers 2.4 billion people across ten countries
claims.source_span: Last month at VivaTech in Paris, we launched our tenth country in the collection, which now represents more than 2.4B people.
claims.note: Source confirms tenth country and more than 2.4B people; 'The Personas dataset' is a fair paraphrase of 'Nemotron-Personas'.

stories.headline: Tune an open-weight agent harness to match frontier model accuracy
stories.summary: LangChain's Deep Agents now supports harness profiles: per-model config files that adjust prompts and add middleware without touching the model. NVIDIA's tutorial shows one middleware fix lifting Nemotron 3 Ultra past a file-pagination failure, closing the gap with proprietary frontier models. Wire a profile to your open-weight deployment and run the built-in evaluation benchmark to confirm it holds.

sections.intro_body: Today's pattern is measurement hygiene: across benchmarks, build tooling, and training data, the shared finding is that what you use to run a model shapes cost and quality as much as the model itself.

sections.name: currents

stories.headline: The New York Fed mapped US bank runs to 1863 with AI
stories.summary: Early signal that language models can unlock historical financial data at scale: the New York Fed screened 374 million digitised newspaper pages, extracting more than 3,000 bank-run episodes from 1863 to 1934. Database is public at finhist.com/bank-runs. Replicated, it becomes a stress-research template; if the extraction carries undetected bias, any stress model calibrated on the count misreads historical volatility.

sections.intro_body: Language models are starting to make century-old financial records queryable at scale. The signal is early and the sourcing is thin, but the direction is clear: unstructured archives are becoming structured risk evidence.
